walkthrough.title: Choose Your Map
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Steam! First things first, the game board is double-sided. One side shows the northeastern USA and Canada, which is ideal for 3 or 4 players. The other side shows Europe's lower Rhine and Ruhr region, which works best for 4 or 5 players. Flip the board to the appropriate side for your player count and lay it flat where everyone can reach.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Goods Bag
walkthrough.text: Grab all 96 Goods cubes: 20 red, 20 blue, 20 yellow, 20 purple, and 16 gray. Dump them all into the cloth Goods Bag and give it a good shake. These cubes represent commodities that you will deliver along your rail networks for points.

walkthrough.title: Populate the Cities
walkthrough.text: Now look at the map. You will see colored city hexes, each with a number printed on them. Draw cubes randomly from the bag and place that many cubes on each city. For example, if a city shows a 3, place three random cubes on it. Important: if you are using the Europe (Ruhr) map with only three players, place one fewer cube on each city.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Goods Supply Spaces
walkthrough.text: Along the edge of the board you will find the Goods Supply spaces. Draw three random cubes from the bag and place them on each Goods Supply space. Again, if playing with only three players, place one fewer cube on each space (two instead of three). These cubes will be used later when someone takes the City Growth or Urbanization actions.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Track Tiles
walkthrough.text: Sort the 136 railway track tiles and place them near the board where everyone can see and reach them. There are many different configurations: straights, gentle curves, tight curves, town tiles, crossings, and complex passing tiles. You do not need to organize them perfectly, but it helps to spread them out so players can find what they need during the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the New City Tiles and City Growth Markers
walkthrough.text: Place the 8 New City tiles next to the map. These come in different colors: 1 red, 1 yellow, 1 purple, 1 blue, and 4 gray. Also place the 9 City Growth markers nearby. New City tiles are used when someone takes the Urbanization action, and City Growth markers track which cities have already received extra goods.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Action Tiles
walkthrough.text: Place the 7 Action tiles face up near the board so everyone can read them. In the Base Game, the numbered side should face up. The tiles are numbered 1 through 7: Turn Order, First Move, Engineer, First Build, City Growth, Locomotive, and Urbanization. Each turn, players will draft one of these tiles for a special ability.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Money Supply
walkthrough.text: Appoint one player as the banker. Set up the money tokens nearby: copper tokens are worth $1, silver tokens are $5, and gold tokens are $10. Players will need money to build track, upgrade locomotives, and pay for certain actions. Your money must remain visible to all other players at all times.

walkthrough.title: Choose Player Colors
walkthrough.text: Each player picks one of the six available colors: orange, green, natural, white, brown, or black. You get 24 tokens of your color. These tokens will mark your track links, your positions on the Income Track, VP Track, Locomotive Level, and Turn Order Display.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Markers
walkthrough.text: Place one of your tokens on the zero space of the Income Track. Place another on the zero space of the Victory Point Track around the edge of the board. Then place one token on the level 1 Locomotive box. Everyone starts with no income, zero VPs, and a level 1 locomotive that can deliver goods across only one link.

walkthrough.title: Determine Turn Order
walkthrough.text: For your first game, we recommend random turn order. Shuffle the Action tiles and deal one to each player. The player who receives the lowest numbered tile goes first. Arrange seating clockwise in ascending order and mark the Turn Order Display. Then return the tiles to the table. Give $1 to the second player, $2 to the third player, and so on as starting cash. For experienced players, you should instead auction each turn order position, paying by moving down the Income Track.

walkthrough.title: Set the Turn Marker
walkthrough.text: Place the turn marker on space 1 of the Game Turn Track. With 3 players you will play 10 turns, with 4 players you play 8 turns, and with 5 players you play 7 turns. Setup is complete. Time to start building railroads!

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Each turn in Steam has six phases, always played in this order: 1) Select Action Tiles, 2) Build Track, 3) Move Goods (two rounds), 4) Income & Expenses, 5) Determine Turn Order, and 6) New Turn. Everyone completes each phase before moving to the next. The game ends after the final turn, and you skip phases 5 and 6 on the last turn. Let's walk through each phase.

walkthrough.title: Phase 1: Select Action Tiles
walkthrough.text: In turn order, each player picks one of the seven Action tiles. These give you a special ability for the turn and also determine next turn's play order. Here they are. Tile 1, Turn Order, means you go first next turn. Tile 2, First Move, lets you move goods before everyone else. Tile 3, Engineer, lets you build 4 track tiles instead of the usual 3. Tile 4, First Build, lets you build track before anyone else. Tile 5, City Growth, lets you add goods cubes to a city for $2. Tile 6, Locomotive, upgrades your locomotive one level for $4 plus the new level cost. Tile 7, Urbanization, lets you turn a town into a city for $6. City Growth and Urbanization both have a Pass Option, meaning you can take the tile but choose not to use it, paying nothing.

walkthrough.title: Phase 2: Build Track
walkthrough.text: In turn order, each player may build up to 3 track tiles on the map (4 if you took Engineer). The first tile you ever build must touch a city hex. After that, you must either extend your existing track or start a new link from any city. Track must connect back to a city through your own track. You cannot extend another player's track. Place one of your tokens on each new link to mark ownership.

walkthrough.title: Track Building Costs
walkthrough.text: Each track tile has a cost based on its edges and the terrain. The base cost is $1 for each edge where track leaves the tile, so a straight tile costs $2 and a complex crossing tile might cost $4. Add $1 if the hex has a town. Add $1 if the hex has a river. Add $2 if the hex is hills. So a straight tile on a hills hex with a river would cost $2 + $2 + $1 = $5. You pay as you build, and if you do not have enough cash, you raise money from the bank at the cost of income.

walkthrough.title: Raising Money
walkthrough.text: When you need money for building, actions, or expenses and do not have enough cash, you raise money from the bank. For every $5 you receive, your Income Track token moves down one space. You must spend your cash on hand first before raising. If you are already at -$10 income, further money raising costs 2 VPs per $5 instead. If you are at -$10 income and 0 VPs, you cannot raise any more money. You can never raise extra money speculatively; only the minimum you need for immediate payments. Any excess from a $5 increment is received as change (up to $4).

walkthrough.title: Towns and Town Track Tiles
walkthrough.text: Some hexes contain towns, shown by a glowing town symbol. You can only place a town track tile on a town hex, and you cannot place a regular tile there. Town track tiles cost the same as other tiles but add $1 for the town. Some town tiles have multiple exits. When you place a town tile, you own any tracks leading from the town unless they connect to another player's existing track. Be careful: if tracks from a town tile create incomplete links, you must extend them next turn or lose ownership.

walkthrough.title: Incomplete Links and Unowned Track
walkthrough.text: If your track does not reach a town or city at both ends by the end of your turn, it is an incomplete link. You keep ownership for now, but you must extend it on your very next turn. If you fail to extend it, you lose ownership and remove your token. Other players can then claim unowned incomplete links by extending them. You can also redirect the last tile of your incomplete link by rotating or replacing it, which costs the non-terrain tile costs again. Redirecting does not count as extending, unless the redirect completes the link.

walkthrough.title: Improving Existing Track
walkthrough.text: Sometimes you need to build through another player's track. You do this by replacing their tile with a more complex one that preserves their existing track and adds yours. For example, replacing a curve with a crossing tile that includes both the original curve and your new straight. You pay for all track on the new tile plus $1 for a town, but you do not pay terrain costs (hills, river) when improving. The improved tile counts as one of your builds for the turn.

walkthrough.title: Urbanization and City Growth (Build Phase)
walkthrough.text: If you took Urbanization and did not pass, you place a New City tile on any town hex during your build phase. Remove any existing track tile first. The New City tile does not count toward your 3-tile build limit. Then pick cubes from one Goods Supply space and place them on the new city along with a City Growth marker. The new city counts as a city for deliveries and link completion. If you took City Growth and did not pass, you pick cubes from one Goods Supply space and place them plus a City Growth marker on any city that does not already have one.

walkthrough.title: Phase 3: Move Goods (Two Rounds)
walkthrough.text: This is the heart of the game. In turn order, each player chooses one activity: improve your locomotive, move a goods cube, or pass. After everyone has done one activity, you go around again for a second activity. If you upgraded your locomotive in the first round, you cannot upgrade it again in the second round of the same turn. If you took the First Move tile, you go before everyone else in each round of this phase.

walkthrough.title: Moving a Goods Cube
walkthrough.text: To deliver a goods cube, pick a cube from any city and move it along completed links to a city that matches the cube's color. The cube must travel at least one of your links, and the number of links it crosses cannot exceed your Locomotive Level. The cube is delivered to the first matching-color city it reaches; you cannot move it past a matching city. You cannot return a cube to its starting city or pass through the same city twice.

walkthrough.title: Using Other Players' Links
walkthrough.text: You can route a delivery through opponents' completed links, but you must use at least as many of your own links as any single opponent's. For example, on a 5-link journey, you could use 3 of yours and 2 of one opponent's, but not 2 of yours and 3 of theirs. You cannot deliver a cube using only other players' links. When you use an opponent's link, they also score track points for their links used.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Track Points
walkthrough.text: When you deliver a cube, you score one track point for each of your completed links that the cube traveled along. Each opponent whose track was used also scores one point per link of theirs that was used. Here is the critical decision: each scoring player must immediately choose to add ALL their points from that delivery to either their Income Track or their VP Track. You cannot split points between income and VPs from the same delivery. The delivering player chooses first, then others choose in turn order.

walkthrough.title: Improving Your Locomotive
walkthrough.text: Instead of moving a cube, you can spend one activity to upgrade your locomotive by one level, for free, during the Move Goods phase. You can only do this once per turn. Your Locomotive Level determines the maximum number of links a cube can travel during delivery: level 1 means one link, level 2 means two links, and so on up to level 6. You can also upgrade by taking the Locomotive Action tile in Phase 1, which costs $4 plus the new level. Taking the tile in Phase 1 and upgrading again in Phase 3 lets you go up two levels in one turn.

walkthrough.title: Phase 4: Income & Expenses
walkthrough.text: In player order, check your Income Track. If your income is positive, collect that much cash from the bank. If negative, you must pay that amount. If you cannot pay, you raise money by moving further down the Income Track or, at -$10, by losing VPs. If you hit -$10 income and 0 VPs and still cannot pay, you are bankrupt and eliminated from the game. Your completed links become unowned.

walkthrough.title: Phase 5: Determine Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Rearrange the Turn Order Display based on the Action tile numbers chosen this turn. The player who chose the lowest-numbered tile goes first next turn, and so on in ascending order. Even if you used the Pass Option on your tile, its number still counts. This is important: choosing a powerful high-numbered action like Urbanization (7) means you will go last next turn. Choosing Turn Order (1) guarantees you go first but gives no special ability this turn.

walkthrough.title: Phase 6: New Turn
walkthrough.text: Return all Action tiles to the table. Advance the turn marker one space. Begin the next turn. On the final turn of the game, skip phases 5 and 6.

walkthrough.title: End of Game and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after the last turn (turn 10 for 3 players, turn 8 for 4, turn 7 for 5). First, convert your income to VPs: gain 1 VP for every 2 points of positive income (round down). If your income is negative, lose 2 VPs per point of negative income. Then remove tokens from incomplete links and score 1 VP per completed link you own. The player with the most VPs wins. Tiebreaker: highest income. Still tied? The player who took the lowest-numbered Action tile on the final turn wins.

summary.title: Turn Structure Overview
bullets: 6 phases per turn: Select Actions, Build Track, Move Goods (x2), Income, Turn Order, New Turn
Game length: 10 turns (3p), 8 turns (4p), 7 turns (5p)
Skip phases 5 & 6 on the final turn

summary.title: Phase 1: Select Action Tiles
bullets: 7 tiles: Turn Order (1), First Move (2), Engineer (3), First Build (4), City Growth (5), Locomotive (6), Urbanization (7)
Tile number determines next turn's player order (low = first)
City Growth ($2) and Urbanization ($6) have Pass Options
Locomotive costs $4 + new level number

summary.title: Phase 2: Build Track
bullets: Build up to 3 tiles per turn (4 with Engineer)
First tile ever must touch a city; after that extend or start from any city
Cannot extend other players' track
Mark each new link with your token

summary.title: Track Building Costs
bullets: Base: $1 per track edge leaving the tile
+$1 for town hex, +$1 for river hex, +$2 for hills hex
Example: straight on hills with river = $2 + $2 + $1 = $5

summary.title: Raising Money
bullets: Receive $5 from bank, move Income Track down 1 space per $5
Must spend cash on hand first; only raise the minimum needed
At -$10 income: lose 2 VPs per $5 raised instead
At -$10 income and 0 VPs: cannot raise more money

summary.title: Towns and Town Track Tiles
bullets: Town hexes require special town track tiles
Town tiles cost +$1 for the town symbol
You own all exits from a town tile unless connected to another player's track
Unextended exits become incomplete links

summary.title: Incomplete Links and Unowned Track
bullets: Track not reaching a city/town at both ends = incomplete link
Must extend on your very next turn or lose ownership
Unowned track can be claimed by any player who extends it
Redirect last tile: pay non-terrain costs again; does not count as extending

summary.title: Improving Existing Track
bullets: Replace simpler tile with complex crossing/passing tile
Must preserve all existing track on the hex
Pay full tile track costs but ignore terrain (hills/river)
Counts as one of your 3 builds for the turn

summary.title: Urbanization and City Growth (Build Phase)
bullets: Urbanization: place New City tile on a town ($6), pick cubes from a Supply space, add City Growth marker
New City tile does NOT count toward your 3-tile build limit
City Growth: add cubes from a Supply space + marker to any city without one ($2)
Cannot add cubes to a city that already has a City Growth marker

summary.title: Phase 3: Move Goods (Two Rounds)
bullets: Each player gets 2 activities per turn (one per round)
Options: upgrade locomotive, move a goods cube, or pass
Can only upgrade locomotive once per turn during this phase
First Move tile holder goes before everyone in each round

summary.title: Moving a Goods Cube
bullets: Move cube from a city to a city matching the cube's color
Max links = your Locomotive Level
Must use at least one of your own links
Cube stops at the first matching-color city; cannot pass through same city twice

summary.title: Using Other Players' Links
bullets: You may route through opponents' completed links
Must use at least as many of your links as any single opponent's
Cannot deliver using only other players' links
Opponents score track points for their links used

summary.title: Scoring Track Points
bullets: 1 track point per link the cube traveled along (for each link owner)
Each scorer must put ALL points from that delivery into income OR VPs, not both
Delivering player chooses first, then others in turn order

summary.title: Improving Your Locomotive
bullets: Free upgrade (1 level) once per turn during Move Goods phase
Locomotive Level = max links per delivery (1-6)
Also possible via Locomotive Action tile ($4 + new level cost) in Phase 1
Can go up 2 levels in one turn by using both methods

summary.title: Phase 4: Income & Expenses
bullets: Positive income: collect cash from bank
Negative income: pay bank (raise money if needed)
Bankruptcy: -$10 income, 0 VPs, can't pay = eliminated
Bankrupt player's completed links become unowned

summary.title: Phase 5: Determine Turn Order
bullets: Rearrange Turn Order Display by Action tile numbers (lowest first)
Pass Option tiles still count their number for ordering
High-number actions = powerful but you go late next turn

summary.title: Phase 6: New Turn
bullets: Return all Action tiles, advance turn marker
Begin next turn at Phase 1

summary.title: End of Game and Final Scoring
bullets: Positive income: +1 VP per 2 income points (round down)
Negative income: -2 VPs per point of negative income
+1 VP per completed link owned; incomplete links score nothing
Tiebreaker: highest income, then lowest Action tile number on final turn

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through a scripted first turn of Steam with three example players: Ron, Morgan, and Bill. Ron is playing black, Morgan is playing orange, and Bill is playing green. We are using the USA/Canada map. Ron is first in turn order, Morgan second, Bill third. Everyone starts at 0 income, 0 VPs, and Locomotive Level 1. Let's see how a turn plays out.

walkthrough.title: Phase 1: Selecting Action Tiles
walkthrough.text: Ron picks first. He wants to build track quickly toward New York, so he takes the First Build tile (4), which lets him build before everyone else. Morgan is next and takes the Engineer tile (3), giving him 4 track tiles instead of 3. Bill wants to urbanize a town into a city, so he takes the Urbanization tile (7) and pays $6. Since Bill has no cash, he raises money: $10 from the bank, moving his income down to -$2. He receives $4 in change.

walkthrough.title: Phase 2: Ron Builds First
walkthrough.text: Ron has the First Build tile, so he builds before Morgan even though Morgan is second in turn order. Ron builds a straight track from Albany toward Poughkeepsie, costing $2 base + $1 for the river = $3. Then he builds a straight town track on Poughkeepsie: $2 + $1 town + $1 river = $4. Finally a straight toward New York: $2 + $1 river = $3. That is $10 total for 3 tiles and 2 completed links. Ron raises $10, dropping his income to -$2, and marks his links with black tokens.

walkthrough.title: Morgan Builds with Engineer
walkthrough.text: Morgan wants to reach New York too, but through a different route. With Engineer, he can build 4 tiles. He builds a gentle curve southwest from Albany on hills ($2 + $2 = $4), then two straights heading south ($2 each), and a gentle curve into New York ($2). That is $10 total for 4 tiles and 1 long link. Morgan also raises money, dropping to -$2 income.

walkthrough.title: Bill Builds and Urbanizes
walkthrough.text: Bill decides to head southeast from Albany. He builds a straight tile ($2) and a gentle curve to Hartford on hills ($2 + $2 = $4), then a gentle curve from Hartford toward New Haven ($2). Then he places a gray New City tile on New Haven. He already paid the $6 cost when he selected Urbanization. He picks cubes from one Goods Supply space and places them on the new city along with a City Growth marker. That is 3 track tiles plus a free New City tile, giving him 2 links.

walkthrough.title: Why Urbanization Was Smart
walkthrough.text: Bill just created a whole new city where there was only a town. New Haven now supplies goods cubes and accepts deliveries of its color. He also completed two links in one turn. The New City tile does not count against his 3-tile building limit, so he got a lot done. The downside? He paid $6 for urbanization plus track costs, so his income took a hit. And because Urbanization is tile 7, Bill will go last next turn.

walkthrough.title: Phase 3, Round 1: Moving Goods
walkthrough.text: Time to deliver! But wait. Everyone has a Locomotive Level of 1, which means cubes can only travel one link. Ron looks at his 2 links from Albany to New York through Poughkeepsie. There is a red cube in Albany and a red city on the map. Can he deliver it in one link? Only if the red city is exactly one link away. Ron checks and finds a blue cube on a city one link from New York. He delivers it to a blue city one link away, scoring 1 track point. He puts it into his income, moving from -$2 to -$1.

walkthrough.title: Morgan's Mistake: Forgetting Locomotive Level
walkthrough.text: Morgan sees a juicy purple cube sitting two links away from a purple city. He announces he is going to deliver it. But hold on! Morgan's Locomotive Level is only 1. He can only move a cube across one link, not two. He cannot make this delivery yet. Morgan needs to upgrade his locomotive before he can make longer deliveries. This is one of the most common mistakes new players make.

walkthrough.title: Morgan Recovers: Upgrading the Locomotive
walkthrough.text: Since Morgan cannot deliver that cube, he uses his first activity to upgrade his locomotive instead. During the Move Goods phase, upgrading costs nothing and scores no VPs. Morgan moves his locomotive token from level 1 to level 2. Now, on his second activity this turn, he will be able to deliver cubes across up to 2 links. Not a wasted turn at all. Remember, you can only upgrade once per turn in this phase.

walkthrough.title: Bill's Move
walkthrough.text: Bill looks at his new city, New Haven. One of the cubes he placed there matches a city one link away. He delivers it, scoring 1 track point. Bill decides to take it as a VP instead of income. His VP marker moves to 1. Good choice? Debatable this early. Income would help pay his negative expenses. Let's see how that plays out.

walkthrough.title: Phase 3, Round 2: Second Activities
walkthrough.text: Each player gets a second activity. Ron delivers another cube for 1 point, taking it as income. Morgan now has a level 2 locomotive and delivers the purple cube across 2 links for 2 track points. He takes both as income, jumping from -$2 to $0. Nice recovery! Bill passes since there are no cubes he can deliver with his level 1 locomotive.

walkthrough.title: Bill's Mistake: Not Upgrading the Locomotive
walkthrough.text: Bill passed on his second activity because he had no deliveries available. But he could have upgraded his locomotive for free instead of passing! Passing gives you nothing. A free locomotive upgrade is always better than passing because it opens up longer delivery routes for future turns. Never pass when you can upgrade. This is a mistake beginners make frequently.

walkthrough.title: Phase 4: Income & Expenses
walkthrough.text: Now everyone collects income or pays expenses. Ron is at -$1 income, so he owes $1 to the bank. He pays from his cash. Morgan is at $0 income, so nothing happens. Bill is at -$2 income and has no cash, so he must raise $5, dropping to -$3 income. He pays $2 and keeps $3 change. Ouch. This is where Bill feels the pain of choosing VP over income earlier. If he had taken that track point as income, he would be at -$1 instead of -$3.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Income vs. VPs Early On
walkthrough.text: In the early game, income is almost always more valuable than VPs. Negative income snowballs because you pay expenses every single turn. One point of income now saves you money for the rest of the game, while one VP is just one VP forever. A good rule of thumb: boost your income to at least $0 before taking VPs. Once your income covers your expenses, then start piling on victory points.

walkthrough.title: Phase 5: Determine Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Action tile numbers determine next turn's order. Ron took First Build (4), Morgan took Engineer (3), Bill took Urbanization (7). Sorted from lowest to highest: Morgan goes first next turn, Ron second, Bill last. Bill paid a steep price for Urbanization: he goes last and his income is deep in the negative. But he has a new city generating delivery opportunities that Ron and Morgan do not have.

walkthrough.title: Phase 6: New Turn
walkthrough.text: Return all Action tiles to the table and advance the turn marker. Turn 1 is complete! In just one turn, you saw track building, urbanization, goods delivery, locomotive upgrades, income management, and turn order jockeying. Every turn in Steam involves these same phases, but the decisions get richer as the network grows and cities fill with goods.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have now seen a full turn of Steam in action. You watched Morgan learn that Locomotive Level limits your deliveries. You saw Bill learn that passing is worse than a free upgrade, and that taking VPs too early can snowball your expenses. These are the kinds of trade-offs that make Steam such a compelling game. You are ready to play on your own!

walkthrough.title: Before You Lay Track
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics like when to take income versus VPs and how to evaluate Action tiles.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every action, terrain type, and game term.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

Follow along to see how a complete turn plays out

Morgan takes Engineer (3) for 4 tiles instead of 3
Bill takes Urbanization (7) for $6, raises money, income drops to -$2

summary.title: Phase 2: Ron Builds First
bullets: First Build lets Ron go before Morgan despite turn order
3 tiles from Albany to New York through Poughkeepsie = $10 total
2 completed links marked with black tokens

bullets: Engineer allows 4 tiles: different route from Albany to New York
Hills hex adds $2 extra cost per tile

Urbanization cost ($6) already paid in Phase 1
New city receives goods cubes from a Supply space + City Growth marker

summary.title: Phase 3, Round 1: Moving Goods
bullets: Everyone at Locomotive Level 1 = cubes can only cross 1 link
Ron delivers a cube 1 link for 1 track point, takes it as income
Score points as income OR VPs, never split from one delivery

summary.title: Morgan's Mistake: Forgetting Locomotive Level

Locomotive Level = max links per delivery; Level 1 = 1 link only

summary.title: Morgan Recovers: Upgrading the Locomotive
bullets: Free upgrade to Level 2 during Move Goods phase
No cost, no VPs, but opens up 2-link deliveries
Only one free upgrade per turn in this phase

summary.title: Bill's Move
bullets: Bill delivers 1 cube from New Haven for 1 track point
Takes it as VP instead of income, VP marker goes to 1
Risky early: income would help pay negative expenses

summary.title: Phase 3, Round 2: Second Activities
bullets: Ron delivers again for 1 income point
Morgan delivers across 2 links with new Level 2 locomotive for 2 income
Bill passes with no deliveries available

summary.title: Bill's Mistake: Not Upgrading the Locomotive
bullets: Bill passed instead of taking a free locomotive upgrade
Passing gives you nothing; upgrading is always better than passing
Free upgrade opens future delivery routes — never waste the opportunity

summary.title: Phase 4: Income & Expenses
bullets: Ron at -$1: pays $1 to bank
Morgan at $0: nothing happens
Bill at -$2 with no cash: raises money, drops to -$3 income

summary.title: Lesson: Income vs. VPs Early On
bullets: Negative income snowballs — you pay expenses every turn
Rule of thumb: get income to $0 before taking VPs
1 income now saves money for the rest of the game; 1 VP is just 1 VP

summary.title: Phase 5: Determine Turn Order
bullets: Sorted by Action tile number: Morgan (3) first, Ron (4) second, Bill (7) last
Higher-number actions are powerful but push you later in turn order
Trade-off is central to the game's strategy

summary.title: Phase 6: New Turn
bullets: Return all Action tiles, advance turn marker
Turn 1 complete — repeat for remaining turns
Decisions get richer as the network grows

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Full turn demonstrated: building, urbanization, delivery, upgrades, income
Mistakes covered: Locomotive Level limits, passing vs. upgrading, VPs vs. income timing
You are ready to play on your own!

summary.title: Before You Lay Track
bullets: Check General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes
Reference the Appendix for quick definitions of actions and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide in the Q&A tab for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Income Before Victory Points
walkthrough.text: The single most important lesson in Steam: get your income to zero or positive before chasing VPs. Negative income means you pay the bank every single turn, which compounds over the course of the game. A player at -$3 income pays $3 per turn for 8 turns, that is $24 in expenses. Meanwhile, a player at +$2 income collects $16 over that same span. The difference is enormous. In the early game, always take delivery points as income until you are at least at zero. Once your income covers your expenses, switch to VPs.

walkthrough.title: Upgrade Your Locomotive Early
walkthrough.text: A level 1 locomotive can only deliver cubes across one link, which severely limits your scoring. Try to reach level 3 by mid-game at the latest. The free upgrade during the Move Goods phase is one of the best deals in the game. Never pass when you could upgrade instead. If you take the Locomotive Action tile in Phase 1 and also upgrade for free in Phase 3, you jump two levels in one turn. Getting to level 4 or 5 opens up massive multi-link deliveries worth 4-5 points each.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Deliveries Before Building
walkthrough.text: Before you lay a single piece of track, look at the cubes on the map. Ask yourself: which cubes can I deliver, and where do they need to go? Build track toward matching-color cities, not just in random directions. A common mistake is building a beautiful network that does not actually connect any cubes to their matching cities. Two short, useful links that enable deliveries are worth far more than one long scenic route to nowhere.

walkthrough.title: Action Tile Selection Strategy
walkthrough.text: Choosing your Action tile is a balance between power and turn order. Low-numbered tiles like Turn Order (1) and First Move (2) give you priority next turn but weak abilities. High-numbered tiles like Locomotive (6) and Urbanization (7) are powerful but push you to the back of the line. In the early game, Engineer (3) and First Build (4) are usually the sweet spot: solid abilities with moderate turn order cost. Take Urbanization only when the new city will generate major delivery opportunities.

walkthrough.title: Watch Other Players' Tracks
walkthrough.text: You can use other players' completed links for your deliveries, as long as you use at least as many of your own links as any single opponent's. This means you do not need to build every link yourself. If an opponent builds a useful link near your network, you can route through it. But be careful: they score track points for their links too. Still, a 4-point delivery where your opponent gets 1 point is much better than not delivering at all.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Overbuild
walkthrough.text: Every piece of track costs money, and money comes from income. Building three tiles every turn sounds productive, but if you are not delivering cubes to recover that investment, you are just digging yourself into debt. Sometimes building one or two tiles and saving cash is the right play. Completed links that enable deliveries pay for themselves. Track that sits idle bleeds money.

walkthrough.title: City Growth and Urbanization Timing
walkthrough.text: City Growth adds cubes to an existing city for $2. Urbanization creates a whole new city for $6. Both are powerful, but timing matters. Use City Growth when a city on your network is running out of cubes to deliver. Use Urbanization when you can place a new city at a strategic junction that opens up multiple delivery routes. The best urbanization plays create a city that multiple players can deliver to, but where your network has the best access.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Scoring Awareness
walkthrough.text: In the final turns, remember that completed links are worth 1 VP each at the end of the game. If you have 10 completed links, that is 10 bonus VPs. Also, your income converts to VPs: positive income gives you 1 VP per 2 income points (rounded down), while negative income costs you 2 VPs per point of negative income. So ending at +$6 income gives you 3 VPs, while ending at -$3 costs you 6 VPs. In the last couple turns, shift your focus from building to maximizing deliveries and cleaning up your income.

Take early delivery points as income; switch to VPs once stable

Take Locomotive tile + free upgrade = 2 levels in one turn
Target level 3-4 by mid-game for efficient multi-link deliveries

bullets: Completed links = 1 VP each at game end (can add 8-12 VPs)
Positive income converts: +1 VP per 2 income (round down)
Negative income penalty: -2 VPs per point of negative income
Last 2 turns: focus on deliveries and income cleanup over building

walkthrough.title: The Income Engine
walkthrough.text: The income engine strategy focuses on building your income as quickly as possible in the first half of the game, then harvesting VPs in the second half. The idea is that once your income is at +$4 or higher, you receive enough cash each turn to pay for track and upgrades without raising money. Every delivery in the early game goes to income. By mid-game, you flip the switch and start dumping all points into VPs. This strategy is consistent and hard to mess up, making it ideal for intermediate players.

walkthrough.title: Building the Income Engine
walkthrough.text: In turns 1-3, take every delivery as income. Build compact, efficient links near cities with lots of cubes. Upgrade your locomotive to level 3 by turn 2. Use the free Phase 3 upgrade each turn. By turn 3 or 4, your income should be +$3 to +$5. Now you can build 2-3 tiles per turn from cash on hand without raising money. The compounding effect is dramatic: while opponents raise money and drop their income, you are collecting cash and building for free.

walkthrough.title: The VP Rush
walkthrough.text: The VP rush is the opposite approach. You aggressively take VPs from deliveries starting on turn 1, accepting deep negative income. This works if you can build very long delivery routes early. With a level 4 or 5 locomotive, a single delivery can be worth 4-5 VPs. The risk is bankruptcy: your income will be deeply negative, and you must carefully manage cash. This strategy rewards experienced players who can calculate exactly how much money they need each turn.

walkthrough.title: Network Positioning
walkthrough.text: Where you build matters as much as what you build. The best network positions are central locations that connect to multiple colored cities, giving you flexibility in deliveries. Avoid building into corners of the map where you have limited directions to expand. Look for city clusters where several cities of different colors sit within 2-3 hexes of each other. These clusters let you deliver cubes efficiently with a low-level locomotive. Also consider building near opponents' networks so you can route through their links for longer deliveries.

walkthrough.title: The Urbanization Play
walkthrough.text: Urbanization is one of the most powerful moves in the game when timed correctly. The ideal urbanization places a new city at a junction where it completes multiple links, creates delivery routes for cubes that were previously stranded, and is colored to match goods that are plentiful nearby. A gray city is especially valuable because gray is the rarest color. The cost of $6 is steep, but if the new city enables 3-4 deliveries over the coming turns, it pays for itself many times over. Watch for towns that sit between two existing networks, creating a bridge city.

walkthrough.title: Turn Order Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Turn order is a hidden weapon in Steam. Going first lets you grab the best cubes before opponents deliver them, and lets you build track to cities before they get blocked. The Turn Order tile (1) guarantees first position next turn but offers no other ability. The real skill is using mid-range tiles strategically. Taking Engineer (3) gives you 4 builds AND a decent turn order. Watch what opponents need. If someone is about to make a critical delivery next turn, going first and delivering that cube yourself can be devastating.

walkthrough.title: Blocking and Competition
walkthrough.text: Steam is an interactive game. You can block opponents by building track that prevents their expansion, by delivering cubes they were planning to deliver, or by urbanizing towns they were heading toward. Building a complex crossing tile through an opponent's planned route forces them to find alternate paths. Taking the First Move tile to steal a delivery before your opponent can reach it is a classic power move. But be careful: over-investing in blocking can damage your own efficiency. Block when it is cheap and when the opponent's loss is greater than your cost.

walkthrough.title: Locomotive Level Timing
walkthrough.text: The optimal locomotive upgrade path depends on your strategy and the map. Level 3 is the minimum for competitive play: it lets you reach most cities within a 3-link radius. Level 4-5 opens up cross-map deliveries worth massive points. Level 6 is rarely worth the investment unless the map specifically rewards very long routes. The Locomotive Action tile costs $4 plus the new level, while the Phase 3 upgrade is free. Always use the free upgrade first. Only take the Locomotive tile when you need to jump two levels in one turn or when the other action tiles do not help you.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Link Scoring
walkthrough.text: Completed links are worth 1 VP each at the end of the game, and this bonus can be the difference between winning and losing. A player with 12 completed links gets 12 free VPs. Build complete links whenever possible. If you have an incomplete link at the end of the game, you score nothing for any tiles in that link. In the final 2-3 turns, prioritize completing any dangling links. Also consider that the income-to-VP conversion at game end can swing 5-10 points: getting income from +$1 to +$4 at the end is worth 2 extra VPs and avoids the negative income penalty.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Board State
walkthrough.text: The best Steam players constantly read the board. Before each turn, ask yourself five questions. Which cubes on the map can I deliver this turn and for how many points? Which cubes might my opponents deliver before I get a chance? Are there any cities running low on cubes that need a City Growth action? Is there a town in a strategic position ripe for Urbanization? What is my cash situation, and can I afford my plans without crippling my income? The answers to these questions should drive every decision you make, from Action tile selection to which links to build.

entries.definition: One of 7 tiles selected each turn during Phase 1. Each tile provides a unique ability and a number that determines next turn's play order. Lower numbers go first next turn.
Guarantees first place in the turn order for the next turn. No special ability this turn. Lowest-numbered action tile.
You move goods before all other players in each round of the Move Goods phase, regardless of your position on the Turn Order Display.
Build up to 4 track tiles during the Build Track phase instead of the usual 3. You still pay normal costs for all tiles built.
You build track before the first player on the Turn Order Display, regardless of your turn order position. Useful for securing key routes.

During Build Track phase, select cubes from one Goods Supply space and add them to any city without a City Growth marker. Costs $2. Has a Pass Option: you may take the tile and choose not to use it, paying nothing.
Immediately upgrade your Locomotive Level by 1. Costs $4 plus the new level number (e.g., upgrading to level 3 costs $4 + $3 = $7). Maximum level is 6. Cannot select if already at level 6.

During Build Track phase, place a New City tile on any town hex. Costs $6. Pick cubes from one Goods Supply space for the new city. Has a Pass Option. The New City tile does not count toward your 3-tile build limit.

Available on City Growth (5) and Urbanization (7) tiles. When you take a tile with a Pass Option, you may choose not to use the action, paying nothing. Flip the tile to its plain side. The tile number still counts for determining next turn's play order.

A section of track connecting one city or town to another city or town. Links are marked with a player's token to show ownership. Completed links are used for goods delivery and score 1 VP at game end.
Track that does not reach a town or city at both ends. You must extend it on your very next turn or lose ownership. Incomplete links cannot be used for goods delivery and score nothing at game end.

Track tiles on the map that have no player's token. Created when a player loses ownership of an incomplete link. Any player can claim unowned track by extending it, as long as they can connect it back to a city through their own track.

Rotating or replacing the last tile of an incomplete link to change its direction. Costs the non-terrain tile costs again (track edges + town, but NOT hills or river). Does not count as extending the link. Only possible on your own or unowned incomplete links.

Replacing an existing track tile with a more complex one (crossing or passing tile) that preserves the original track and adds new track. You pay for all track on the new tile plus town cost, but not terrain costs. Counts as one of your builds.
A special track tile bearing a town symbol. Can only be placed on a hex containing a town. Some have 1 exit, others have 2-4 exits. You own all exits unless they connect to another player's track.
A track tile with multiple track segments crossing or passing through the same hex. Used to improve existing track or build multiple tracks on one hex. Has 4 exits, costing $4 base (plus terrain).
Any hex that is not a hills hex or a city hex. Includes coastal hexes. No terrain surcharge for building. Printed in green on the map.
A hex covered in brown hills/mountains art with a white border. Adds +$2 to the cost of placing a track tile. This surcharge is waived when improving or redirecting track.
Depicted as blue water with dark edges on a hex. Includes canals and smaller lakes. Adds +$1 to the cost of placing a track tile on that hex. Waived when improving or redirecting.

A hex that is partly land and partly water. Treated the same as a plains hex for building purposes. Coastal water outlined in white is impassable; track may not cross a hex side with a heavy black line.

A fully colored hex printed on the board or a hex containing a New City tile. Cities supply and receive goods cubes. Track is considered to run to every edge of a city hex, so cities connect to adjacent track in all directions.

A hex with a glowing town symbol. Towns do not supply or receive goods cubes unless urbanized into a city. Only town track tiles can be placed on town hexes. Towns can be urbanized into cities using the Urbanization action.
An impassable border on the map. Track cannot cross or run into a hex side marked with a heavy black line. Represents impassable water, mountain ranges, or the board edge.

Colored wooden cubes (red, blue, yellow, purple, gray) representing commodities. Placed on cities during setup. Delivered along track links to matching-color cities for track points. Removed from the game once delivered.
Red cubes that can only be delivered to red cities. 20 in the supply.
Blue cubes that can only be delivered to blue cities. 20 in the supply.
Yellow cubes that can only be delivered to yellow cities. 20 in the supply.
Purple cubes that can only be delivered to purple cities. 20 in the supply.
Gray cubes that can only be delivered to gray cities. 16 in the supply. Gray cities only appear via New City tiles through the Urbanization action, making gray goods harder to deliver.
Spaces along the edge of the board holding cubes for City Growth and Urbanization. Filled with 3 random cubes during setup (2 for 3-player games). Never refilled during the game.
The cloth bag holding all goods cubes. Used during setup to randomly draw cubes for cities and supply spaces. Delivered cubes return to the bag. Never draw from the bag during the game itself.

Tracked on the Locomotive Level boxes (1-6). Determines the maximum number of completed links a goods cube can travel during a delivery. Everyone starts at level 1. Can be upgraded via the Locomotive action tile or a free upgrade during Move Goods phase (once per turn).

A track on the board showing your income from positive values down through zero to -$10. Positive income means you collect cash each turn. Negative income means you pay the bank each turn. Moves up when you take delivery points as income, moves down when you raise money from the bank.
The scoring track around the edge of the board (0-50+). Records your current VP total. Can go above 50 using a +50 marker. The player with the most VPs at game end wins.

When you need cash for payments, receive $5 from the bank and move your Income Track token down 1 space per $5. Must spend cash on hand first. Only raise the minimum needed. At -$10 income, raising money instead costs 2 VPs per $5. Cannot raise money speculatively.

Points scored when a goods cube is delivered. Each player scores 1 track point per completed link of theirs that the cube traveled along. Must be assigned entirely to income or entirely to VPs for each delivery (cannot split).

If you are at -$10 income, 0 VPs, and cannot pay a required expense, you are bankrupt and eliminated. Your tokens are removed; completed links become unowned. Remaining players can claim your incomplete links.

One of 8 tiles (1 red, 1 yellow, 1 purple, 1 blue, 4 gray) placed on town hexes via Urbanization. The new city receives goods cubes from a Supply space and a City Growth marker. Functions as a normal city for all purposes.

A marker placed on a city when it receives extra cubes from City Growth or when a New City tile is placed. A city with a marker cannot receive City Growth again. New City tiles have a marker printed on them.

A display on the board showing the current player order. Updated each turn in Phase 5 based on Action tile numbers. Lower numbers go first. Critical for planning: high-numbered actions mean going last next turn.
A track showing the current turn number. The turn marker advances one space each turn. Game length: 10 turns (3 players), 8 turns (4 players), 7 turns (5 players).
Colored wooden tokens (24 per player). Used to mark owned track links, positions on the Income Track, VP Track, Locomotive Level, and Turn Order Display.
The recommended starting version of Steam (pages 3-13 of the rulebook). Players raise money as needed during any phase, and Action tiles have fixed costs. Simpler than the Standard Game.

The advanced version of Steam (pages 14-15). Three key differences: money can only be raised in Phase 1 (Buy Capital), turn order is auctioned every turn with real money bids, and locomotive maintenance costs $1 per level each turn. More punishing cash management.
Phase 1 of the Standard Game. Players raise all money they will need for the turn. Cannot raise money during any other phase. Must plan all expenses in advance. Very easy to leave yourself short.

Phase 2 of the Standard Game. Players bid cash for turn order positions. 1st and 2nd place pay full bid. Last place pays nothing. Others pay half (rounded up). Replaces the fixed Action tile cost system.
In the Standard Game, you pay $1 per Locomotive Level each turn during Income & Expenses. A level 6 locomotive costs $6 per turn in maintenance. If you cannot pay, lose 1 VP per $2 short.
At game end, positive income converts to VPs: +1 VP per 2 income points (round down). Negative income penalizes: -2 VPs per point of negative income. Zero income = no change.
At game end, each completed link you own is worth 1 VP. Incomplete links score nothing. Remove tokens from incomplete links before scoring.
If VPs are tied, the player with the highest income wins. If still tied, the player who took the lowest-numbered Action tile on the final turn wins.
One side of the double-sided game board. Covers the northeastern United States and southeastern Canada. Recommended for 3-4 players.
The other side of the game board. Covers Europe's lower Rhine and Ruhr region. Recommended for 4-5 players. With 3 players, reduce cubes on each city and supply space by 1.
